sampleEnriched.title: Found a Stripe Access Token, posing a risk to payment processing services and sensitive financial data.
sampleEnriched.description: A hardcoded Stripe access token is committed in config/secrets.env (line 5). Anyone with repository or git-history access can call the Stripe API with it to access payment and financial data. Committed secrets persist in history even after the line is deleted.
sampleEnriched.remediation: Immediately revoke/rotate the token in the Stripe dashboard. Remove secrets.env from the repo, add it to .gitignore, and load secrets from environment variables or a secret manager (Vault, AWS Secrets Manager). Purge the value from git history with git filter-repo or BFG.

sampleEnriched.title: express@4.16.0: express vulnerable to XSS via response.redirect()
sampleEnriched.description: express 4.16.0 is affected by GHSA-qw6h-vgh9-j6wx: untrusted input passed to response.redirect() may lead to XSS. Exploitation is conditional and requires attacker-controlled redirect input plus user interaction.
sampleEnriched.remediation: Upgrade express to >= 4.20.0 and regenerate package-lock.json. Additionally, validate any redirect target against an explicit allowlist.

sampleNew.title: No authentication or authorization on any route, including RCE sinks
sampleNew.description: None of the routes (/ping, /calc, /whoami) enforce authentication or authorization. Combined with the shell-exec sink in /ping and the eval sink in /calc, any anonymous client can trigger OS command execution and arbitrary code execution. This broken-access-control design flaw is not modeled by the pattern scanners, which only flag the injection sinks in isolation.
sampleNew.remediation: Add authentication middleware (session/JWT/API key) and per-route authorization checks with deny-by-default. Remove or tightly restrict privileged operations and run the service under a least-privilege account.

sampleNew.title: AWS access key ID disclosed via unauthenticated /whoami endpoint
sampleNew.description: GET /whoami returns the hardcoded AWS access key ID in its JSON response to any unauthenticated caller (server.js:31). Returning cloud credential material through a public API leaks the identifier half of a key pair and aids credential enumeration; the same key is hardcoded in source, so its secret half is also at risk. This is a design-level sensitive-data-exposure issue distinct from the SAST secret finding.
sampleNew.remediation: Never return credentials or key identifiers in API responses. Remove the key from source, load it from a secret manager, and gate the endpoint behind authentication/authorization.

sampleNew.title: Fragment of AWS secret access key written to startup logs
sampleNew.description: At startup the app logs the first characters of the AWS secret access key (server.js:34). Writing any portion of a secret to stdout/log aggregation risks leaking sensitive material to logging systems and lowers the value's effective entropy.
sampleNew.remediation: Do not log secrets or fragments of them. Remove the interpolation; if a startup marker is needed, log a non-sensitive constant.
